Altimeter & Barometric.clue: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is defined by ICAO as this specific value in inches of mercury.
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: The standard atmosphere defines sea-level pressure as 29.92 inHg (1013.25 hPa). This is the baseline for pressure altitude and flight level assignments.
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inHg

Altimeter & Barometric.clue: The aneroid capsule inside the altimeter case is surrounded by this pressure, and the capsule expands or contracts as it changes with altitude.
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: Static pressure from the static port fills the altimeter case. The aneroid capsule is sealed — as static pressure drops with altitude, the capsule expands; as it rises with descent, the capsule compresses.
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Altimeter aneroid capsule responds to static pressure changes

Altimeter & Barometric.clue: Before flight, this value in inches of mercury must be entered in the altimeter's Kollsman window so the instrument correctly reads altitude above sea level.
choices: Ram air pressure from the pitot tube
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: The local altimeter setting (QNH) is dialed into the Kollsman window to reference the altimeter to local atmospheric pressure, giving an accurate MSL altitude reading.
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Altimeter must be set to local barometric pressure before flight

Altimeter & Barometric.clue: The altimeter measures barometric altitude by sensing changes in this pressure through an aneroid capsule and converting expansion to a feet or meters reading.
choices: Total pressure from both pitot and static
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: The altimeter uses only static pressure — sensed through the static port — feeding an aneroid capsule that expands as static pressure decreases with altitude increase.
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Altimeter measures barometric altitude via static pressure changes

Altimeter & Barometric.clue: An altimeter reading 500 feet higher than field elevation on the ground most likely has this problem with the Kollsman window setting.
choices: The altimeter setting is too high
The altimeter setting is too low
The aneroid capsule has a leak
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: If the altimeter setting (Kollsman window) is higher than actual local pressure, the instrument over-reports altitude. High setting = high reading. Remember: 'High to low, look out below.'
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Incorrect Kollsman setting causes altitude errors

Altimeter & Barometric.clue: When transitioning from a high-pressure area to a low-pressure area, the altimeter will indicate this if the pilot doesn't update the setting.
Altimeter & Barometric.explanation: Flying from high to low pressure without updating the altimeter setting, the instrument reads higher than actual altitude. 'High to low, look out below' — the aircraft is lower than indicated.
Altimeter & Barometric.learningObjective: Pressure changes cause altimeter errors if setting not updated

Airspeed Indicator.clue: This forward-facing probe senses and delivers ram air pressure — the key input to the airspeed indicator and air data computer.
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: The pitot tube collects ram (impact) air pressure and delivers it to the ASI and ADC. The pressure increases with airspeed, enabling airspeed computation.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: Pitot tube senses and delivers ram air pressure to airspeed indicator

Airspeed Indicator.clue: The airspeed indicator works by measuring the difference between ram air pressure from the pitot tube and this second pressure from the static port.
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: The ASI is a differential pressure instrument — it measures the difference between pitot (ram) pressure and static pressure. The resulting differential equals dynamic pressure, which corresponds to airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: ASI measures differential between pitot and static pressure

Airspeed Indicator.clue: When a pitot tube becomes completely blocked, this flight instrument is primarily affected because it needs ram air pressure to calculate airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: The ASI uses the differential between ram (pitot) pressure and static pressure. A blocked pitot tube eliminates the ram pressure input, making the ASI unable to measure airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: Blocked pitot tube primarily affects airspeed indicator

Airspeed Indicator.clue: During descent, a blocked pitot tube causes the ASI to read falsely high because static pressure increases while this pressure remains trapped and constant.
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: In descent, static port pressure rises. The trapped ram pressure stays constant. The ASI sees an increasing pitot-minus-static differential and registers a falsely high airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: Blocked pitot during descent causes falsely high ASI reading

Airspeed Indicator.clue: During a climb with a blocked pitot tube, static pressure decreases while this pressure stays constant, causing the ASI to show falsely increasing airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: During climb, atmospheric pressure falls. Blocked pitot traps ram pressure constant. ASI measures pitot minus static — as static decreases, the differential grows, reading falsely high airspeed.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: Blocked pitot during climb causes falsely increasing ASI reading

Airspeed Indicator.clue: An airspeed indicator that consistently reads lower than actual aircraft speed most likely has this type of fault in the pitot pressure system.
choices: Partial pitot tube blockage or leak in pitot plumbing
Airspeed Indicator.explanation: A consistent low reading means less ram pressure is reaching the ASI than expected. A partial blockage (reduced flow) or a leak (pressure escaping) in the pitot line both reduce effective ram pressure.
Airspeed Indicator.learningObjective: Consistently low ASI reading indicates partial pitot blockage or leak

VSI & Rate of Climb.clue: This pitot-static instrument displays the rate of change of barometric altitude in feet per minute by sensing how quickly static pressure is changing.
VSI & Rate of Climb.explanation: The VSI uses a differential between a direct static port connection and a calibrated metering orifice to measure how fast static pressure is changing, giving a climb/descent rate in fpm.
VSI & Rate of Climb.learningObjective: VSI displays rate of change of barometric altitude

VSI & Rate of Climb.clue: In level flight, the VSI pointer should indicate zero. If the pointer rests slightly off zero on the ground, the pilot should use this feature to correct it.
choices: The zero-adjust screw on the VSI face
VSI & Rate of Climb.explanation: A small zero-adjust screw on the VSI face allows the pilot or tech to mechanically reposition the pointer to zero when the aircraft is stationary on the ground. This is a normal preflight check.
VSI & Rate of Climb.learningObjective: VSI zero-adjust screw corrects zero-point offset

VSI & Rate of Climb.clue: The VSI achieves its rate indication by routing static pressure through this precision device that creates a controlled delay in pressure equalization.
VSI & Rate of Climb.explanation: The VSI's calibrated metering orifice creates a controlled 'lag' — direct static pressure changes immediately on one side of the diaphragm, while the other side equalizes slowly through the orifice, creating a pressure differential that indicates climb/descent rate.
VSI & Rate of Climb.learningObjective: VSI uses a calibrated orifice to delay pressure equalization

VSI & Rate of Climb.clue: The VSI is sometimes called a 'trend instrument' because it has this inherent characteristic that causes a delay in reading during the start of a climb or descent.
VSI & Rate of Climb.explanation: The VSI's calibrated orifice creates an inherent 6-9 second lag when entering or exiting a climb or descent. The instrument trend is reliable even during the lag period, hence 'trend instrument.'
VSI & Rate of Climb.learningObjective: VSI has inherent lag of 6-9 seconds

VSI & Rate of Climb.clue: A VSI consistently shows a rate of descent in level flight because this fault allows static pressure to equalize faster than the instrument's calibrated metering orifice.
choices: A leak in the static system
VSI & Rate of Climb.explanation: A static system leak provides a path for pressure to equalize faster than the VSI's calibrated orifice. The VSI senses the higher pressure influx as an apparent descent, even in level flight.
VSI & Rate of Climb.learningObjective: VSI showing descent in level flight indicates static system leak

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: Among primary flight instruments, this directional reference instrument requires no gyro or power source because it aligns directly to Earth's magnetic field.
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: The magnetic compass uses Earth's magnetic field — not a gyro. It works without electrical or vacuum power, making it the ultimate backup directional reference.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Magnetic compass is not gyroscopically driven

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: These two flight instruments are commonly powered by the aircraft vacuum system, which spins their gyro rotors via air jets.
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: The AI and DG (heading indicator) are conventionally vacuum-driven. The ASI, altimeter, and VSI are pitot-static; the TC is typically electrically driven.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Attitude indicator and DG are vacuum-powered

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: In a vacuum-driven attitude indicator, air is drawn through this component by the vacuum system and directed at rotor buckets to spin the gyro.
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: The vacuum system draws filtered air through a nozzle aimed at buckets machined around the rotor rim; the jet of air impinges on the buckets to spin the gyro.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Vacuum system spins gyro rotor via air through nozzle

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: Unlike the turn-and-bank indicator, this instrument senses both roll rate and yaw rate because its gyro is canted at approximately 30 degrees.
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: The turn coordinator's gyro is tilted at ~30° so it responds to both yaw and roll. The turn-and-bank indicator has a vertically mounted gyro that only senses yaw.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Turn coordinator senses roll rate and yaw rate; turn-and-bank senses only yaw rate

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: This gyroscopic property allows the attitude indicator's rotor to maintain a fixed orientation in space, resisting aircraft pitch and roll movements to provide a stable reference.
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: Rigidity in space (also called gyroscopic inertia) causes a spinning rotor to resist any force changing its axis orientation. This resistance provides the stable reference needed for the attitude indicator.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Gyroscopic rigidity in space allows stable attitude reference

Gyros & Vacuum System.clue: Gyroscopic precession causes a force applied to a spinning gyro to produce a reaction that occurs 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. This is why a heading indicator has this operational requirement.
choices: It must be periodically reset to the magnetic compass heading
It requires pitot heat to function
It can only be used above 500 feet AGL
It does not work in turbulence
Gyros & Vacuum System.explanation: Precession causes the directional gyro to gradually drift from the correct heading over time. Pilots must periodically reset the DG to match the magnetic compass — typically every 15 minutes.
Gyros & Vacuum System.learningObjective: Gyroscopic precession requires periodic DG resetting to magnetic compass

AHRS & Glass Cockpit.clue: In a glass-cockpit aircraft, this computer processes pitot-static pressure data to supply airspeed and altitude information to the Primary Flight Display.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.explanation: The ADC interfaces directly with the pitot-static system and converts pressure measurements into digital airspeed and altitude data for the PFD.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.learningObjective: ADC as source of airspeed and altitude for PFD

AHRS & Glass Cockpit.clue: These solid-state devices, used in modern EFIS attitude sensing, measure angular rate and linear acceleration without any rotating or moving mechanical components.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.explanation: EFIS systems use MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) sensors — solid-state gyroscopes and accelerometers — to determine attitude and motion without mechanical moving parts.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.learningObjective: EFIS uses MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers for attitude data

AHRS & Glass Cockpit.clue: In the standard 'Basic T' instrument layout, the attitude indicator occupies this position, with the airspeed indicator to its left and the altimeter to its right.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.explanation: The Basic T puts the AI at upper-center — the reference instrument for all cross-checks — with ASI upper-left, ALT upper-right, and DI/HI lower-center.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.learningObjective: Standard T arrangement of flight instruments

AHRS & Glass Cockpit.clue: The AHRS in a modern EFIS determines attitude, heading, and angular rates using these three types of solid-state sensors instead of spinning mechanical gyros.
choices: GPS receivers, barometric sensors, and radar altimeters
Pitot probes, static ports, and angle-of-attack vanes
MEMS chips, ADC units, and flux gate compasses only
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.explanation: AHRS systems combine rate gyros (angular rate), accelerometers (linear motion/gravity), and magnetometers (magnetic heading) in solid-state form — no mechanical spinning gyros needed.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.learningObjective: AHRS uses rate gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers — no mechanical gyros

AHRS & Glass Cockpit.clue: Unlike traditional gyro systems, modern AHRS requires this initialization process after power-up before attitude and heading data becomes valid for flight.
choices: Manual compass swing to set deviation card
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.explanation: AHRS units need an alignment period after power-up. The system must be stationary so accelerometers can find gravity (level) and magnetometers can sense magnetic north. This takes 30-90 seconds depending on the system.
AHRS & Glass Cockpit.learningObjective: AHRS requires alignment period after power-up

Static System & Troubleshooting.clue: Flush-mounted static ports supply ambient atmospheric pressure to these three flight instruments: the altimeter, VSI, and one more.
Static System & Troubleshooting.explanation: The static pressure system feeds three instruments: the altimeter (altitude reference), the VSI (rate of pressure change), and the airspeed indicator (static side of differential pressure).
Static System & Troubleshooting.learningObjective: Static port supplies altimeter, VSI, and ASI

Static System & Troubleshooting.clue: These three flight instruments all rely on an accurate static port reading: the altimeter, the VSI, and this third instrument.
Static System & Troubleshooting.explanation: The ASI uses static pressure as the reference side of its differential measurement. Along with the altimeter and VSI, all three require a clean, accurate static port connection.
Static System & Troubleshooting.learningObjective: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI rely on static port

Static System & Troubleshooting.clue: When the primary static ports are blocked (such as by ice), many aircraft provide this emergency feature that connects the instruments to an interior pressure source.
Static System & Troubleshooting.explanation: The alternate static source valve connects the instruments to cabin static pressure. Cabin pressure is slightly lower than outside due to airflow, so altimeter reads slightly high and ASI reads slightly high with alternate static selected.
Static System & Troubleshooting.learningObjective: Alternate static source provides emergency backup when ports are blocked

Static System & Troubleshooting.clue: A blocked static port causes these two instruments to become unusable: one freezes at the altitude where the blockage occurred, and the other reads zero continuously.
Static System & Troubleshooting.explanation: Both the altimeter and VSI rely on static pressure changes. A blocked static port traps pressure — the altimeter freezes at the blocked altitude, and the VSI reads zero (no pressure change detected).
Static System & Troubleshooting.learningObjective: Blocked static port freezes altimeter and VSI at zero

Static System & Troubleshooting.clue: During a static system leak check per 14 CFR 91.411, the system must hold vacuum without leaking more than this amount over one minute to pass inspection.
Static System & Troubleshooting.explanation: Per FAR 91.411, the static system must not leak more than 100 feet of altitude equivalent in one minute when tested under vacuum. This ensures instrument accuracy for IFR flight.
Static System & Troubleshooting.learningObjective: Static system leak check tolerance is 100 feet per minute

final.clue: In an EFIS architecture, a failure of the Air Data Computer (ADC) will result in red 'X's over which combination of primary flight instruments?
final.explanation: The ADC processes pitot-static information. A failure blanks out the Airspeed Indicator, Altimeter, and Vertical Speed Indicator. Attitude and Heading are handled by the AHRS.

final.clue: A modern AHRS unit uses this specific sensor combination to align to magnetic north without requiring a physical spinning flux valve.
final.explanation: Modern AHRS uses a 3-axis solid-state magnetometer (usually mounted in the wing to avoid interference) to electronically sense the Earth's magnetic field and derive magnetic heading.

final.clue: If an aircraft takes off with the static port taped over, how will the airspeed indicator react during the climb?
choices: It will lock at the takeoff speed
final.explanation: With a trapped static pressure from takeoff altitude (which is higher pressure than at altitude), the pitot-static differential is smaller than it should be. The ASI will read falsely low in a climb.
